title: The Managed Hand: Race, Gender, and the Body in Beauty Service Work By Miliann Kang University of California Press. 2010. 328 pages. $27.60 paper
abstract: The Managed Hand by Miliann Kang is a beautifully designed and brilliantly executed study of the body work performed in Korean-owned nail salons in New York City. Drawing on extensive fieldwork in six nail salons of varying class and racial compositions, interviews and casual conversations with owners, employees and patrons, the book challenges a number of powerful stereotypes and myths: the “model minority” status of Asian immigrants, the harmonious unity of women and the innocuousness of feminized spaces like nail salons, beauty parlors and cosmetic “clinics.” Kang's skillful analysis of her research sites not only highlights the unequal power relations within them, but also situates these inherently “local” settings within a complex backdrop of global and structural forces (of migration, capitalist expansion, nationalism and market competition). In so doing, it makes an important contribution to the growing literature on “mundane” beauty practices, as well as to scholarship dealing more generally with the “body/work nexus.”
